news_summary: Today's positive environmental news highlights support for new farmers in Michigan, encouraging climate success stories, and the critical importance of protecting rapidly changing oceans for humanity's future.
news_summary_fr: Les bonnes nouvelles environnementales d'aujourd'hui mettent en avant le soutien apporté aux nouveaux agriculteurs du Michigan, les initiatives climatiques couronnées de succès, ainsi que l'importance cruciale de protéger les océans, qui subissent des changements rapides, pour l'avenir de l'humanité.
news_summary_es: Las noticias positivas de hoy sobre el medio ambiente destacan el apoyo a los nuevos agricultores de Míchigan, las iniciativas climáticas que están dando buenos resultados y la importancia fundamental de proteger los océanos, que están cambiando rápidamente, para el futuro de la humanidad.

articles.title: Becoming a farmer is hard. This Michigan program wants to help.
articles.content:
This coverage is made possible through a partnership between Grist and Interlochen Public Radio in northern Michigan.

As the U.S. faces an aging farmer population, communities are looking for ways to shore up the next generation of growers. But high upfront costs, access to land, and a shifting climate can make entry into the field feel out of reach for many people looking to get into the business.

Tucked on farmland at the southern edge of Traverse City, Michigan, one program wants to solve some of these problems by letting aspiring farmers learn by doing.

The Great Lakes Incubator Farm attracts students from all over the country. Over the course of seven months, a three-student cohort learns about topics like pest management, how to drive a tractor, and what to include in a farm business plan.

“Nobody gets into farming for sane reasons, other than the sanity of knowing where your food comes from and just general health,” said Rachel Greenberg, a 33-year-old student farmer from Indianapolis. “The challenges are pretty never-ending.”

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Farm bankruptcies were up 46 percent last year, according to a National Farm Bureau report. As land prices have risen due to demand from developers, more than 50,000 acres of farmland have been lost in the last two decades, research has found.

Despite the headwinds, the student farmers said they’re driven by wanting to know where their food comes from, to contribute to local communities, and to teach others to do the same.

The farm training program — a project of the Grand Traverse Conservation District — has fewer economic pressures than running a farm business, Greenberg said. The fruits and vegetables that students grow will go to local residents who have already committed to buying the season’s produce, and leftovers will be donated to food-rescue operations. Unlike a traditional business, the goal isn’t to make a profit.

“The whole incubator idea is something you see a lot in the world of entrepreneurship, and it’s beautiful that somebody saw that and was like, ‘Why don’t we just do that with farming?’” Greenberg said.

To support our nonprofit environmental journalism, please consider disabling your ad-blocker to allow ads on Grist. Here's How

Troy Saruna, 28, said at a time when climate change is driving more severe weather, he wants to better understand his impact on the natural world. Saruna worked in conservation around the country prior to the program and has no farming experience.

The training program focuses on teaching regenerative agriculture, a method of farming that focuses on soil health and reduces the amount of heat-trapping gases released into the atmosphere.

Student farmer Shanaya Holmes of Alabama poses by a row of spinach at the Great Lakes Incubator Farm in northern Michigan.

Vivian La / IPR News

“Our food systems are just so inextricably tied to the health of the planet,” Saruna said. “I’m just really interested in striking up a new balance where I can understand, interpret, and just develop some new instincts in terms of feeding myself and having thriving communities that also support wildlife.”

Farmers with some experience also find the program helpful to more deeply develop their skills. Shanaya Holmes, 49, runs a small 4-acre farm in Alabama.

She’s looking to learn how to grow food in a different climate than the South and to improve her record-keeping — tracking what’s been planted, what soil was used, or how much money was spent on equipment.

“It’s a challenge to switch that button off to come inside and do bookwork, bookwork, bookwork when you’re so used to outside, outside, outside,” she said.

Adam Brown, the farm’s manager and instructor, said the farmer training program is meant to be a stepping stone.

“It’s really built for anybody who can then filter out and work anywhere in the food system, either manage a farm, start their own business, or any rung of that ladder where people can just help out in the food system,” Brown said.

Brown, whose background is in ecology, wouldn’t have pursued farming himself if it wasn’t for a similar training program he did 15 years ago on the West Coast.

“I can pay it forward, my lessons, and all the wisdom that I learned throughout my years of farming, and be a mentor to these other people, and I feel like it’s super important,” he said.

The training program, now in its second year, is one of the only of its kind in northern Michigan, according to data from Michigan State University. Around the country, there are roughly 100 similar programs, according to a group at Tufts University that coordinates a national network of training farms, though no comprehensive list exists.

The Great Lakes Incubator Farm relies mostly on a nearly $700,000 federal grant from the U.S. Department of Agriculture aimed at supporting beginner farmers. That grant ends after the harvest season in October. Brown plans to reapply for USDA funding again this year but said he’s looking for backup options because of how competitive the grant program is.

In 2025, the USDA canceled $148 million in grants — including some in the beginner farmer program — to comply with President Donald Trump’s early executive orders targeting climate action, environmental justice, and diversity, equity, and inclusion.

Adam Brown sits on a tractor at the Great Lakes Incubator Farm. Vivian La / IPR News

Brown said that besides the USDA grant, there aren’t many large pools of money available that support efforts to train the next generation of farmers. (The Great Lakes Incubator Farm is also supported by some state grants.)

Lack of consistent funding is a big reason there aren’t more of these training programs, said Jon LaPorte, a farm business management educator for Michigan State University Extension, which put together a beginner farmer’s guide in partnership with the USDA last year.

“It’s almost like a double-edged sword that they’re trying to help people get started, but then they’ve got the same struggles of staying sustainable themselves,” he said.

That means even as the share of young people in farming grows, programs to support them might be harder to come by, LaPorte said. In Michigan, farmers under the age of 45 increased by about 20 percent between 2017 and 2022, according to the USDA’s census. Sustaining that growth will be a challenge, he said.

“Because of those hurdles, they don’t all stay in, and what we want to see is more of those people being able to stay in, having more farms, more diversity of farms,” LaPorte said. “More people involved in agriculture at that level is really, really important.”

Brown, the farm manager, said students in his training program learn that the growing season doesn’t always go smoothly — and challenges, like frost damage on plants, are just part of the job.

“This is a great space for failure too, right? Because there’s not a whole lot of risk here,” he said. “It’s a perfect, experimental type of atmosphere.”
articles.reasoning: The article describes a meaningful program that supports the next generation of farmers through hands-on training in regenerative agriculture, addressing challenges like climate change, farmland loss, and aging farmer populations. It highlights broad societal benefits including sustainable food production, environmental health, and community support. The detailed description of the program's structure, goals, and impact provides substantial context, making it an inspiring story of environmental and agricultural progress.
articles.personality_title: Michigan program trains new farmers to grow food and protect the land
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – In the United States, many farmers are getting older, and fewer young people are starting farms. New farmers face big challenges like high costs, difficulty finding land, and changing weather caused by climate change.

**What happened** – In northern Michigan, the Great Lakes Incubator Farm offers a seven-month training program where groups of three students learn farming skills by working on real farmland. The program teaches things like pest control, tractor driving, and how to plan a farm business. It focuses on regenerative agriculture, which helps keep soil healthy and reduces harmful gases in the air.

**Impact** – This program helps new farmers gain experience without the usual financial risks of running a farm. The food grown is sold to local buyers or donated to food rescue groups. It supports farmers from different backgrounds, including those with no experience and others looking to improve their skills. The program also encourages caring for the environment while growing food.

**What's next step** – The program depends mostly on a federal grant that ends in October, and the farm manager plans to apply for funding again but is also looking for other support because grants are competitive. Growing more programs like this could help more young farmers stay in agriculture despite the challenges.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A hands-on farm training program in Michigan is helping new farmers learn sustainable farming while supporting local communities and the environment.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un programme du Michigan forme de nouveaux agriculteurs à cultiver et protéger la terre
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Aux États-Unis, de nombreux agriculteurs vieillissent et peu de jeunes commencent des fermes. Les nouveaux agriculteurs font face à de grands défis comme les coûts élevés, la difficulté à trouver des terres et les changements climatiques.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Dans le nord du Michigan, la ferme incubatrice Great Lakes propose un programme de formation de sept mois où des groupes de trois étudiants apprennent le métier directement sur une ferme. Le programme enseigne la gestion des parasites, la conduite de tracteur et la planification d’une ferme. Il met l’accent sur l’agriculture régénératrice, qui préserve la santé du sol et réduit les gaz nocifs.

**Impact** – Ce programme permet aux nouveaux agriculteurs d’acquérir de l’expérience sans les risques financiers habituels. Les aliments produits sont vendus localement ou donnés à des associations. Le programme aide des agriculteurs de divers horizons, qu’ils soient débutants ou expérimentés, et encourage à respecter l’environnement.

**Prochaine étape** – Le programme dépend surtout d’une subvention fédérale qui se termine en octobre. Le responsable compte refaire une demande de financement et cherche aussi d’autres soutiens, car les subventions sont compétitives. Développer plus de programmes similaires pourrait aider davantage de jeunes à rester dans l’agriculture.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un programme de formation pratique dans le Michigan aide de nouveaux agriculteurs à apprendre une agriculture durable tout en soutenant les communautés locales et l’environnement.
articles.personality_title_es: Programa en Michigan capacita a nuevos agricultores para cultivar y cuidar la tierra
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – En Estados Unidos, muchos agricultores son mayores y pocos jóvenes comienzan en la agricultura. Los nuevos agricultores enfrentan grandes desafíos como altos costos, dificultad para conseguir tierras y cambios climáticos.

**Qué pasó** – En el norte de Michigan, la granja incubadora Great Lakes ofrece un programa de siete meses donde grupos de tres estudiantes aprenden a cultivar en una granja real. El programa enseña control de plagas, manejo de tractor y planificación de negocios agrícolas. Se enfoca en la agricultura regenerativa, que mantiene la salud del suelo y reduce gases dañinos.

**Impacto** – Este programa ayuda a los nuevos agricultores a ganar experiencia sin los riesgos financieros normales. Los alimentos producidos se venden localmente o se donan a organizaciones. Apoya a agricultores con diferentes niveles de experiencia y promueve el cuidado del medio ambiente.

**Próximo paso** – El programa depende principalmente de una subvención federal que termina en octubre. El encargado planea solicitar fondos nuevamente y buscar otras opciones porque las subvenciones son competitivas. Más programas así podrían ayudar a que más jóvenes permanezcan en la agricultura.

**Resumen en una frase** – Un programa práctico en Michigan ayuda a nuevos agricultores a aprender agricultura sostenible mientras apoyan a sus comunidades y al medio ambiente.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a small group of young farmers’ silhouettes working together in a lush northern Michigan vegetable field, surrounded by rows of growing spinach and other produce, with a vintage tractor nearby and a rustic farmhouse in the background, all rendered in soft natural earth tones and greens that evoke hope, growth, and community support.

articles.title: Climate success stories: A pleasant surprise
articles.summary: We have experiences, inherited knowledge, and approaches that escape the comprehension of the global north, and the more we hear each other’s stories, the better chances we have of surviving the climate crisis.
articles.content:
When the Global Voices team first broached the idea of covering positive climate stories in May, I was apprehensive. I couldn’t imagine that there were enough positive environmental stories in the global majority for us to manage our target of 10 articles, let alone surpass it. Our Human Perspectives on AI spotlight in April exceeded all expectations, both in the number of pieces we got and the response to them, so I was even more worried.

But then the pitches began to come in, and stories began to be written. Every day, I saw our tracking spreadsheet grow: communities fighting to save and restore rivers, people coming up with amazing solutions to counter specific effects, a focus on the importance of seeds and food production. Kenya, the Dominican Republic, Nepal, Mozambique, Tuvalu, Yemen, Brazil — people are fighting the good fight all over the world.

Positive change is happening

The story that really brought home the importance of this coverage for me was this one about the partial restoration of the Aral Sea in Central Asia. I was in my early thirties when I first found out about the ecological devastation of the Aral Sea, and I was in shock for a few months, often telling people, “But did you know it’s GONE? We used to draw it on maps, and it’s GONE!?” So reading that it is being successfully revived was truly a moment of wonder. In a year (no, decade) of seemingly endless bad news, it felt like a gift to read about this. And it happened in a region so ignored by the news in general, with action from the state and help from a favoured villain in mainstream English news: China.

That right there is the core importance of telling these stories from the global majority, for the global majority — something we hold very dear at Global Voices. The modern world, while magically connecting us, manages to isolate us.

It makes sense that we are so tied to the internet and social media, because it allows us to see and hear things we simply could not offline (like Global Voices stories). You could not read a print newspaper from Cameroon in Dhaka, but you can read it online. Yet the internet also somehow manages to isolate us in our bubbles by presenting us exclusively with what it thinks we want, and we so rarely see the communities fighting the same battles as we are in the global majority unless we actively seek them out. This is why Global Voices has committed to prioritising collaborative stories, and this one on successful conservation efforts around the world really reminded me of the many ways we are all still fighting (and winning) the fight.

And it’s important to recognise that progress is being made. People are not in fact trapped on a road to inevitable doom, even — especially — in the chronically under-resourced and usually dismissed global majority. Because, as our Climate Justice Editor Sydney Allen put it in her May editorial, “It is important to share replicable, scalable examples of successful climate action because that’s the only way these strategies can be employed in other communities that need them.”

It’s an obvious thing to say, but this spotlight really brought home how important it is to come together as communities to fight our battles and hope to win them. From the Indigenous women in Bolivia organising to fight wildfires and the Indigenous Torwali people of Pakistan trying to save the Swat River, to the Filipinos filing a landmark suit against an oil company and Kenyans reclaiming their land, when people come together, they create movements much more powerful than the sum of their parts.

It’s not just the people — sometimes it’s the state

Another aspect that this coverage brought to my attention was that governments are not, in fact, entirely ignoring the problems of the climate crisis. Governments all across Central Asia are responding to the annual air pollution crisis (we’re still waiting for this to happen in India, where every year turns the north into a smog-filled hellscape), covered in a story from our excellent partners over at Vlast.kz.

That progress in Kazakhstan is driven by the government, as are many of the steps being taken in Barbados to reshape the fishing industry, including passing legislation, implementing recovery plans, and collecting data. The Pacific island nation of Tuvalu, which is facing the very real possibility of losing all its land to rising ocean levels, has begun to build the framework to become a “digital nation” — some might call this a gimmick, but it is perhaps the only way to preserve a national identity in the face of the inadequate response to this global crisis.

Women front and centre

It also stood out to me how many women’s groups, communities, and collectives are involved in this restorative work. Broadly speaking, women suffer the most from the ill effects of climate change. Women also tend to be entrusted with caring for and fostering community, since it is a kind of emotional, interpersonal and domestic labour. It is no surprise that women end up fighting on the frontlines of this work. In fact, French feminist and ecologist Françoise d’Eaubonne “argued that the oppression of women and the exploitation of nature stemmed from a system deeply rooted in patriarchal values.”

I’m quoting our Narrative Analysis Editor Samanta Azpurua, who dived into Ugandan ecofeminism, exploring the narrative that “Women are essential to Uganda’s climate action” for her piece this month. Using the Civic Media Observatory methodology, she pulls up some important context on the history of the feminist and conservation movements in Uganda. That piece is what I call a very GV story: it makes it impossible to ignore the immense intersectionality of life in the global majority. Climate, feminism, Indigenous knowledge, technology used to curtail rights, capitalism, and postcolonialism all have a role to play.

We are always stronger in solidarity

At the end of the day, it’s the people of the global majority who are and will be the hardest hit by the climate crisis. We are also the people who benefit the most from communication and alliances between global majority nations and civil society groups. We have experiences, inherited knowledge, and approaches that escape the comprehension of the global north, and the more we hear each other’s stories, the better chances we have of surviving the climate crisis.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights multiple positive, real-world climate actions and successes across diverse global majority communities, showcasing significant environmental restoration, government involvement, and grassroots activism with broad societal impact. It provides detailed examples and emphasizes solidarity and replicable solutions, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news with meaningful scope and substance.
articles.personality_title: Communities worldwide lead hopeful climate action with real success
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Many people hear mostly bad news about climate change, especially from poorer countries often called the global majority. These countries face big challenges but also have important stories of fighting back and making progress.

**What happened** – A project by Global Voices collected and shared positive climate stories from places like Kenya, Nepal, Bolivia, and Central Asia. These stories include restoring the Aral Sea, protecting rivers, fighting wildfires, changing fishing laws, and building new digital plans to save island nations. Women’s groups and local communities are often leading these efforts. Governments in some places, such as Kazakhstan and Barbados, are also taking steps to help.

**Impact** – These stories show that progress is possible even in places with fewer resources. They reveal how sharing knowledge and working together can help other communities learn and try similar solutions. The involvement of women and Indigenous people highlights how different groups contribute important ideas and action. This approach challenges the idea that climate change is only a problem with no hope.

**What’s next step** – The Global Voices team plans to keep sharing these positive stories to inspire more cooperation between communities. Governments and organizations may use these examples to create better climate plans. By learning from each other, more places might find ways to protect their environment and people.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Communities around the world are making real progress against climate change by sharing ideas, working together, and leading change locally and nationally.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des communautés du monde entier mènent des actions climatiques porteuses d’espoir
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Beaucoup de personnes n’entendent que des mauvaises nouvelles sur le changement climatique, surtout venant des pays plus pauvres appelés la majorité mondiale. Ces pays font face à de grands défis mais ont aussi des histoires importantes de lutte et de progrès.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une initiative de Global Voices a rassemblé et partagé des histoires positives sur le climat venant de pays comme le Kenya, le Népal, la Bolivie et l’Asie centrale. Ces histoires parlent de la restauration de la mer d’Aral, de la protection des rivières, de la lutte contre les incendies, de changements dans les lois sur la pêche, et de nouveaux projets numériques pour sauver des îles. Les groupes de femmes et les communautés locales sont souvent à l’avant-garde. Des gouvernements, comme ceux du Kazakhstan et de la Barbade, prennent aussi des mesures.

**Impact** – Ces histoires montrent que le progrès est possible même dans des endroits avec peu de ressources. Elles montrent que partager des connaissances et travailler ensemble peut aider d’autres communautés à apprendre et à essayer des solutions similaires. L’implication des femmes et des peuples autochtones souligne que différents groupes apportent des idées et des actions importantes. Cela remet en question l’idée que le changement climatique est un problème sans espoir.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe de Global Voices prévoit de continuer à partager ces histoires positives pour encourager plus de coopération entre communautés. Les gouvernements et organisations pourraient utiliser ces exemples pour créer de meilleurs plans climatiques. En apprenant les uns des autres, plus d’endroits pourraient trouver des moyens de protéger leur environnement et leurs habitants.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des communautés du monde entier réalisent des progrès concrets contre le changement climatique en partageant des idées, en travaillant ensemble et en menant des changements locaux et nationaux.
articles.personality_title_es: Comunidades de todo el mundo lideran acciones climáticas esperanzadoras con éxitos reales
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Muchas personas solo escuchan malas noticias sobre el cambio climático, especialmente de países más pobres llamados la mayoría global. Estos países enfrentan grandes desafíos, pero también tienen historias importantes de lucha y progreso.

**Qué pasó** – Un proyecto de Global Voices reunió y compartió historias positivas sobre el clima de lugares como Kenia, Nepal, Bolivia y Asia Central. Estas historias incluyen restaurar el Mar de Aral, proteger ríos, combatir incendios, cambiar leyes de pesca y crear planes digitales para salvar naciones insulares. Grupos de mujeres y comunidades locales suelen liderar estos esfuerzos. Algunos gobiernos, como los de Kazajistán y Barbados, también están tomando medidas.

**Impacto** – Estas historias muestran que el progreso es posible incluso en lugares con pocos recursos. Revelan cómo compartir conocimientos y trabajar juntos puede ayudar a otras comunidades a aprender y probar soluciones similares. La participación de mujeres y pueblos indígenas destaca cómo diferentes grupos aportan ideas y acción importantes. Esto desafía la idea de que el cambio climático es solo un problema sin esperanza.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo de Global Voices planea seguir compartiendo estas historias positivas para inspirar más cooperación entre comunidades. Gobiernos y organizaciones podrían usar estos ejemplos para crear mejores planes climáticos. Al aprender unos de otros, más lugares podrían encontrar formas de proteger su medio ambiente y a su gente.

**Mensaje en una frase** – Comunidades de todo el mundo están logrando avances reales contra el cambio climático al compartir ideas, trabajar juntas y liderar cambios a nivel local y nacional.
articles.image_prompt: A vibrant, detailed painting of a flourishing Aral Sea surrounded by diverse hands of different skin tones gently nurturing sprouting seeds and flowing rivers, with stylized silhouettes of women and community figures in traditional attire symbolizing global unity and climate resilience, rendered in warm, earthy tones and soft natural colors.

articles.title: Humanity’s future depends on protecting the rapidly changing ocean
articles.summary: The ocean covers more than 70 per cent of the planet and regulates climate, sustains biodiversity, and supports economies and cultures worldwide. It’s the foundation of life on Earth.
articles.content:
However, it has been under duress for some time and going forward faces multiple threats which not only gravely endanger its future health but the future of humanity itself.

Some 550 experts from 86 countries have spent almost five years compiling a 1600-page assessment detailing the challenges the ocean faces. This scientific guide delivers the knowledge humankind needs to protect and sustain the planet.

It’s called the World Ocean Assessment, and here’s what those 1600 pages reveal.

The ocean matters to everyone, everywhere

The ocean shapes everyone’s daily life even if they do not live in coastal zones.

It stabilizes the climate by absorbing most of the planet’s excess heat as well as damaging greenhouse gases. Without its cooling effect, more extreme weather can be expected which will threaten food systems and supply chains, and insurance markets.

It serves as food supply. When fish stocks collapse or supply chains break due to climate impacts or illegal fishing, prices rise, not just for seafood, but for many foods that rely on global trade and coastal economies.

It provides mental and physical health benefits, medicines, and a significant share of breathable oxygen.

The ocean supports trillions of dollars in global trade, tourism, and jobs.

The ocean is under intensifying stress

Humans are reshaping marine ecosystems. The global population reached 8.2 billion in 2024, with 37 per cent of those people living within 100 km of the coast.

© Ocean Image Bank/Dipayan Bose

Inevitably, this has concentrated human and economic activity in vulnerable coastal zones, increasing the extraction of natural resources, infrastructure expansion, waste discharge, and habitat degradation.

At the same time, offshore development is intensifying, with wind farms, deepwater oil infrastructure, and expanding seabed cables and pipelines altering habitats farther from shore.

Climate change is transforming conditions

Data relating to ocean warming and sea level rise is dramatic.

The rate of sea level rise, due to melting ice caps and temperature-driven water expansion, has doubled from up to1.9 mm/year before 2015 to 4.3 mm/year in 2023.

Arctic temperatures are rising four times faster than the global average.

Hypoxic (or dead) zones, where oxygen levels are so low that most marine life cannot survive, now span 4.5 million km².

16 per cent of the total increase in ocean temperatures since 1955 has occurred after 2018.

© Ocean Image Bank/Vivek Mehra

Biodiversity is declining across nearly every marine habitat

Marine life is under severe stress, reflected in the approximately 80 per cent decline in Caribbean coral reefs since the 1970s. Ninety per cent of global coral reefs may disappear if warming exceeds 1.5°C above industrial levels.

Critical coastal ecosystems, like mangroves and seagrass, continue to shrink.

Species from plankton to marine mammals are shifting towards the North and South Poles as temperatures rise, while non-indigenous species are spreading more easily under altered environmental conditions.

Pollution is widespread and increasing

Marine pollution is intensifying.

Each year, 52 million tonnes of plastic waste enter the ocean, contributing to an estimated 24 trillion microplastic particles, which are now known to affect more than 4,000 marine species.

Chemical contamination is also rising, with over 4,000 pharmaceutical and personal care compounds detected in marine waters.

The good news? Some legacy pollutants such as mercury have declined in a few regions.

Ocean food systems are threatened

Marine food systems are a vital source of nutrition and livelihoods, providing 20 per cent of animal protein consumed by humans globally.

© Ocean Image Bank/Naja Bertolt

Marine aquaculture continues to expand and has become a global $90 billion industry. In addition, 121 million people engage in marine recreational fishing, contributing to local economies and well-being.

Yet the stability of these systems is increasingly at risk:

37 per cent of fish stocks were overfished in 2021.

Illegal, unreported, and unregulated fishing removes an estimated 8-14 million tonnes annually, generating $9–17 billion in illicit revenue.

Disease outbreaks, pollution, and climate stress continue to challenge the long-term sustainability of marine aquaculture and fisheries.

The ocean economy is large but not yet sustainable

The ocean economy is valued at $1.5 trillion per year and projected to exceed $3 trillion by 2030.

Coastal and marine tourism supports 174 million jobs.

© Unsplash/Omar Eagle

Efforts are being made to understand the ongoing impacts and sustainability of offshore oil and gas production as well as shipping which transports over 80 per cent of world trade and contributes to global greenhouse gas emissions.

Governance and knowledge

International cooperation on ocean governance is gaining momentum, but the 57 global treaties relating to ocean protection is resulting in a fragmented approach.

Achieving a sustainable ocean economy requires equity and the prominent inclusion of the knowledge and traditional practices of indigenous communities. Without them ocean health, community wellbeing, and sustainable and equitable development will be more difficult to achieve.

Large gaps persist in ocean knowledge, with only 27 per cent of the seafloor mapped as of 2025, leaving deep sea ecosystems, biological processes, and cumulative impacts poorly understood.

Solutions abound

Despite mounting pressures, solutions exist, including nature-based approaches, emissions reduction, and expanded marine protection.

However, even the full restoration of ocean ecosystems would contribute only around two per cent of global climate mitigation targets, underscoring the need for systemic change.

The coming decade is decisive: without rapid, coordinated global action, ocean health will continue to decline, threatening climate stability, biodiversity resilience, food security, livelihoods and the wellbeing of billions.
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article presents a comprehensive scientific assessment highlighting the critical importance of ocean health for climate stability, biodiversity, and global economies, while emphasizing existing threats and the urgent need for coordinated global action and sustainable solutions. It provides substantial context and details about the ocean's role, current challenges, and potential solutions, impacting billions worldwide and inspiring hope through identified pathways for protection and sustainability.
articles.personality_title: New global report shows how protecting oceans secures our future
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – The ocean covers over 70% of Earth’s surface and plays a key role in controlling the climate, providing food, and supporting jobs. But it is facing many problems caused by human activities and climate change.

**What happened** – A group of 550 experts from 86 countries spent almost five years creating a 1,600-page report called the World Ocean Assessment. This report explains the challenges the ocean faces, like warming waters, rising sea levels, pollution, and loss of marine life.

**Impact** – The ocean helps keep the planet cool by absorbing heat and greenhouse gases. It also provides 20% of the animal protein people eat and supports millions of jobs in fishing, tourism, and trade. However, overfishing, plastic pollution, and warming seas are harming ocean health, which threatens food supplies and economies worldwide.

**What's next step** – The report highlights the need for fast, worldwide cooperation to protect the ocean. Solutions include reducing pollution, creating more marine protected areas, and using nature-based methods to restore habitats. Scientists also call for better ocean knowledge and including indigenous communities in decisions.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Protecting the ocean through global teamwork and sustainable actions is essential for maintaining climate balance, food security, and millions of livelihoods.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau rapport mondial montre comment protéger les océans assure notre avenir
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L'océan couvre plus de 70 % de la surface de la Terre et joue un rôle clé dans le contrôle du climat, la fourniture de nourriture et le soutien à l'emploi. Mais il fait face à de nombreux problèmes causés par les activités humaines et le changement climatique.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Un groupe de 550 experts de 86 pays a passé près de cinq ans à créer un rapport de 1 600 pages appelé l'Évaluation mondiale de l'océan. Ce rapport explique les défis auxquels l'océan est confronté, comme le réchauffement des eaux, la montée du niveau de la mer, la pollution et la perte de la vie marine.

**Impact** – L'océan aide à garder la planète fraîche en absorbant la chaleur et les gaz à effet de serre. Il fournit aussi 20 % des protéines animales consommées par les humains et soutient des millions d'emplois dans la pêche, le tourisme et le commerce. Cependant, la surpêche, la pollution plastique et le réchauffement des mers nuisent à la santé des océans, ce qui menace les approvisionnements alimentaires et les économies dans le monde.

**Prochaine étape** – Le rapport souligne la nécessité d'une coopération mondiale rapide pour protéger l'océan. Les solutions incluent la réduction de la pollution, la création de zones marines protégées et l'utilisation de méthodes basées sur la nature pour restaurer les habitats. Les scientifiques appellent aussi à une meilleure connaissance des océans et à l'inclusion des communautés autochtones dans les décisions.

**Conclusion en une phrase** – Protéger l'océan grâce à un travail d'équipe mondial et à des actions durables est essentiel pour maintenir l'équilibre climatique, la sécurité alimentaire et des millions de moyens de subsistance.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo informe global muestra cómo proteger los océanos asegura nuestro futuro
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El océano cubre más del 70 % de la superficie de la Tierra y juega un papel clave en controlar el clima, proveer alimentos y apoyar empleos. Pero enfrenta muchos problemas causados por actividades humanas y el cambio climático.

**Qué pasó** – Un grupo de 550 expertos de 86 países pasó casi cinco años creando un informe de 1,600 páginas llamado Evaluación Mundial del Océano. Este informe explica los desafíos que enfrenta el océano, como el calentamiento del agua, la subida del nivel del mar, la contaminación y la pérdida de vida marina.

**Impacto** – El océano ayuda a mantener el planeta fresco al absorber calor y gases de efecto invernadero. También proporciona el 20 % de la proteína animal que comen las personas y sostiene millones de empleos en pesca, turismo y comercio. Sin embargo, la sobrepesca, la contaminación plástica y el calentamiento del mar están dañando la salud del océano, lo que amenaza el suministro de alimentos y las economías en todo el mundo.

**Próximo paso** – El informe destaca la necesidad de una cooperación rápida a nivel mundial para proteger el océano. Las soluciones incluyen reducir la contaminación, crear más áreas marinas protegidas y usar métodos basados en la naturaleza para restaurar hábitats. Los científicos también piden mejorar el conocimiento del océano e incluir a las comunidades indígenas en las decisiones.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Proteger el océano mediante trabajo en equipo global y acciones sostenibles es esencial para mantener el equilibrio climático, la seguridad alimentaria y millones de medios de vida.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vast ocean gently cradling vibrant coral reefs and diverse marine life, with symbolic silhouettes of interconnected fishing nets and delicate marine plants like seagrass and mangroves weaving through the water, all beneath a calm sky showing faint outlines of wind turbines and seabed cables on the horizon, embodying harmony between ocean health, human stewardship, and sustainable future.
